section.title: The Temptation in the Wilderness
section.sectionRationale: Immediately after the Father declares Jesus His beloved Son, the Spirit leads Him into the wilderness to be tested by the devil. Three times the tempter attacks, and three times Jesus answers with Scripture. Where Adam fell in a garden and Israel failed in the desert, the true Son stands firm, proving Himself the faithful Israel and the obedient Son before His ministry unfolds.
section.thesis: In the wilderness the beloved Son is tested to the limit and, by trusting and obeying the word of God, defeats the tempter at every point, succeeding where Adam and Israel failed, and refusing every shortcut to glory that bypasses the path of obedient suffering.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the Spirit leads Jesus to be tested after forty days of fasting; the temptation to turn stones to bread; the temptation to leap from the temple; the temptation to gain the kingdoms by worshiping Satan; and the devil's defeat.

verses.text: Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be {{peirazo|tempted}} by the {{diabolos|devil}}.
After fasting forty days and forty nights, He was hungry.

units.label: Bread from stones
units.note: man shall not live on bread alone

verses.text: The tempter came to Him and said, “If You are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.”
But Jesus answered, “{{gegraptai|It is written}}: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.’”

units.label: The pinnacle of the temple
units.note: do not put the Lord to the test

verses.text: Then the devil took Him to the holy city and set Him on the pinnacle of the temple.

“If You are the Son of God,” he said, “throw Yourself down. For it is written: ‘He will command His angels concerning You, and they will lift You up in their hands, so that You will not strike Your foot against a stone.’”
Jesus replied, “It is also written: ‘Do not put the Lord your God to the test.’”

units.label: All the kingdoms of the world
units.note: worship the Lord your God and serve Him only

verses.text: Again, the devil took Him to a very high mountain and showed Him all the kingdoms of the world and their glory.
“All this I will give You,” he said, “if You will fall down and worship me.”
“Away from Me, Satan!” Jesus told him. “For it is written: ‘{{proskyneo|Worship}} the Lord your God and serve Him only.’”
Then the devil left Him, and angels came and ministered to Him.

peirazo.gloss: To test, try, tempt. The same word covers a testing meant to prove and a tempting meant to destroy. The Spirit leads Jesus to be tested, and the devil means it for ruin, yet the trial proves the Son faithful.

diabolos.gloss: Devil, slanderer, accuser. The personal enemy of God and His people, also named Satan in the passage. He tempts with half-truths and even misused Scripture, seeking to divert the Son from the Father's path.

gegraptai.gloss: It stands written, a perfect form meaning it was written and remains in force. Jesus meets each temptation with this settled authority, answering all three times from Deuteronomy, the very book Israel had in the wilderness.

proskyneo.gloss: To worship, bow down, do homage. The devil demands the worship due to God alone; Jesus answers that worship and service belong to the Lord only, refusing to win the world by any means but the Father's.

basic.context: Straight from the joy of His baptism, Jesus is led by the Spirit into the wilderness and tested by the devil for forty days. Weak with hunger, He faces three temptations: to make bread from stones, to leap from the temple and force God to catch Him, and to gain all the world's kingdoms by worshiping Satan. Each time Jesus answers with a verse from Deuteronomy, and the devil finally leaves.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this against the background of the Old Testament. Jesus' forty days recall Israel's forty years in the desert, and He quotes the very passages Israel learned there. Where Israel grumbled, tested God, and turned to idols, Jesus trusts, obeys, and worships God alone. He is the true Israel and the faithful Son, undoing the pattern of failure.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus is tested, a word that can mean both a trial to prove and a temptation to destroy; the Spirit intends the first, the devil the second. Each answer begins it is written, a settled, standing authority. And Jesus insists that worship belongs to God alone, refusing the devil's offer to gain the world by bowing to him.
basic.typology: Jesus is the new Adam, tempted as Adam was but standing firm where Adam fell. He is the true Israel, forty days in the wilderness matching forty years, succeeding where the nation failed. And He is the obedient Son, tested as God's son Israel was tested, proving faithful to the Father's word and way.
basic.christ: The whole passage displays Christ's perfect obedience and His victory over the devil. He refuses every shortcut to messianic glory, choosing the Father's path of suffering over the tempter's easy crown. His triumph here begins the rescue of a people held captive by the one He defeats, and His sinless obedience is part of the righteousness He wins for us.
basic.god: God is worthy of exclusive worship and trust. His word is the food by which His people truly live, and it is not to be twisted or put to the test. The Father sends His Son into the trial and sustains Him, and afterward sends angels to minister to Him, showing His care in the midst of testing.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 8:3, 6:16, and 6:13 are the three texts Jesus quotes. Genesis 3 tells of Adam's fall to a similar temptation. Hebrews 4 says Jesus was tempted in every way yet without sin. And James 4 urges believers to resist the devil, who will flee.
basic.oneStory: In the wilderness the long story of human failure meets its turning point. Where Adam fell and Israel faltered, the beloved Son stands firm, armed with nothing but the word of God and unwavering trust in His Father. By refusing the tempter's shortcuts, Jesus sets His face toward the cross, the true and costly road to the kingdom, and begins to reclaim the world the devil claimed to own.

context.literary: The temptation is paired deliberately with the baptism: the Son declared in chapter 3 is at once tested in chapter 4, and both scenes turn on His identity as Son of God. The three temptations build to a climax on the mountain, and the whole account is framed by the Spirit leading Him in and angels ministering at the end. It prepares for the Sermon on the Mount, where the obedient Son teaches the way of the kingdom.
context.historical: The Judean wilderness was a harsh, desolate region, a fitting place of testing. Forty days of fasting deliberately echoes Moses on the mountain and Israel's forty years. The pinnacle of the temple overlooked a steep drop; the high mountain and the kingdoms of the world evoke the sweep of imperial power. The devil's misuse of Psalm 91 shows temptation can wear the clothing of Scripture.
context.canonical: All three of Jesus' replies come from Deuteronomy 6 to 8, Israel's wilderness instruction, framing Jesus as the true Israel who passes the test the nation failed. The scene reverses the fall of Genesis 3 and grounds the New Testament's confidence that Jesus, tempted like us yet sinless, is a sympathetic and victorious high priest, Hebrews 4:15.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the beloved Son, tested to the limit, remains perfectly faithful to God's word and worship, defeating the devil and refusing every shortcut to glory, and so proving Himself the true Israel and obedient Son who will save His people the Father's way.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The unique, once-for-all testing of the Messiah is described, not prescribed; we cannot reenact His wilderness trial. Yet His method is instructive: He meets temptation with Scripture, trust, and exclusive worship of God, a pattern the New Testament commends to every believer.

debates.question: Were the temptations physical events or a vision?

views.label: Real, bodily temptations
views.case: Matthew narrates concrete movements, hunger, the temple pinnacle, a mountain, and treats them as genuine external assaults the Son truly resisted in the flesh.

views.label: Partly visionary in mode
views.case: Some hold that seeing all the kingdoms of the world at once suggests a visionary element, though the reality and force of the temptations are in no way diminished.

debates.bearing: Whether experienced bodily or in vision, the temptations were real and really resisted; Jesus' sinless victory stands either way.

debates.question: What was the deepest aim of the three temptations?

views.label: To seize messianic glory without the cross
views.case: Bread, spectacle, and kingdoms offer the crown by shortcut, tempting Jesus to bypass suffering; each answer keeps Him on the Father's path that leads through the cross.

views.label: To make the Son distrust and test the Father
views.case: The refrain if You are the Son of God targets Jesus' trust in the Father's word just spoken at the baptism, tempting Him to prove or doubt rather than simply trust.

debates.bearing: Both aims are one: to divert the Son from faithful obedience; Jesus defeats them by trusting the Father and refusing any road to glory but His.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus is led up to be tested, peirazo, a word poised between a trial meant to prove and a temptation meant to destroy; the Spirit intends proving, the devil, diabolos, the slanderer, intends ruin. Three times Jesus answers with gegraptai, it stands written, the settled and abiding authority of God's word, and each quotation is from Deuteronomy, Israel's own wilderness scripture. At the summit the enemy demands proskyneo, worship, that belongs to God alone, and Jesus refuses to gain the world by any road but the Father's. The vocabulary frames the whole as the faithful Son passing the test His people failed.

typology.fulfillment: Where Adam fell to temptation in the garden, Jesus stands firm in the wilderness, beginning to undo the fall, Romans 5:19.

typology.type: The true Israel in the wilderness
typology.fulfillment: Forty days answering Israel's forty years, Jesus trusts and obeys where the nation grumbled and rebelled, quoting Deuteronomy 6 to 8.

typology.type: The obedient Son who refuses false worship
typology.fulfillment: Tested as God's son Israel was, Jesus worships God alone, the faithfulness that qualifies Him to save His people.

study.christ: Here Christ is revealed as the sinless, obedient Son and the conqueror of the devil. Hungry and weak, He nonetheless holds fast to the Father's word and refuses every shortcut to the glory that is rightfully His. He will not turn stones to bread to serve Himself, will not force the Father's hand in a spectacle, and will not receive the kingdoms from the tempter's hand, because He has resolved to receive them from the Father by the way of the cross. His victory in the wilderness is the first skirmish in the war that ends at Calvary and the empty tomb, and His flawless obedience is woven into the righteousness He gives His people.
study.god: The God of this passage is the only proper object of worship and the sure ground of trust. His word is the true bread by which His people live, a word not to be twisted, as the devil twists Psalm 91, nor put to the test, as Israel tested Him at Massah. The Father does not spare His Son the trial; He leads Him into it by the Spirit and sustains Him through it, then sends angels to minister to Him. In the testing of the Son we see a God who is faithful in the furnace and who provides for those who trust Him.

crossReferences.note: Man does not live on bread alone but on every word from God, Jesus' first reply.
The fall of Adam to temptation, reversed by the faithful obedience of the last Adam.
Jesus was tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin, our sympathetic high priest.
Resist the devil and he will flee from you, as he departs from Jesus here.

study.oneStory: The wilderness testing is a hinge in the whole story of salvation. Adam fell, and Israel failed, but now the beloved Son stands in the same kind of trial and does not yield, armed only with trust in His Father and the word that stands written. By turning down every shortcut the devil offers, Jesus commits Himself to the long, costly road of obedience that runs to the cross. His victory begins the liberation of a world in bondage and moves the story toward the day when the tempter is finally crushed.

quiz.q: Who led Jesus into the wilderness, and why?

options.text: The Spirit led Him there to be tested by the devil.
options.note: Verse 1 says the Spirit led Him to be tempted by the devil.

options.text: Jesus withdrew there on His own to fast and pray.
options.note: He did fast, but the text says the Spirit led Him there for the purpose of testing.

options.text: The devil dragged Him there against His will.
options.note: The Spirit led Him; the devil came to tempt, but did not compel Him into the wilderness.

options.text: John the Baptist sent Him into the desert.
options.note: It was the Spirit, not John, who led Jesus into the wilderness.

quiz.q: How did Jesus respond to each temptation?

options.text: He answered each time with Scripture, quoting from Deuteronomy.
options.note: All three replies begin It is written and cite Deuteronomy 6 to 8.

options.text: He rebuked the devil with His own divine authority.
options.note: He does finally say Away from Me, Satan, but His consistent weapon was the written word of God.

options.text: He performed a miracle to prove His power.
options.note: He refused to work miracles at the devil's prompting; He answered with Scripture.

options.text: He remained silent and simply endured.
options.note: He spoke, answering each temptation with a specific verse.

quiz.q: What did the third temptation offer, and what was its price?

options.text: All the kingdoms of the world, if Jesus would fall down and worship Satan.
options.note: Verses 8-9 record the offer of the kingdoms in exchange for worshiping the devil.

options.text: Rescue by angels, if He would leap from the temple.
options.note: That was the second temptation; the third offered the kingdoms for worship of Satan.

options.text: Bread and comfort, if He would end His fast.
options.note: That relates to the first temptation; the third concerned the world's kingdoms.

options.text: Long life, if He would abandon His mission quietly.
options.note: The offer was the kingdoms of the world in exchange for worship, not long life.

quiz.q: How does Jesus succeed where Adam and Israel failed?

options.text: As the true Israel and new Adam, He trusts and obeys God's word under testing.
options.note: His forty days and Deuteronomy quotations frame Him as the faithful Son who does not fall.

options.text: He simply had more willpower than they did.
options.note: It was faithful trust in and obedience to God's word, not mere willpower, that won the day.

options.text: He avoided temptation altogether by leaving the wilderness.
options.note: He did not avoid it; He faced and defeated it there.

options.text: He was never really tempted, only appearing to be.
options.note: The temptations were real; Hebrews says He was tempted in every way, yet without sin.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus refuse the devil's shortcuts to glory?

options.text: He chose the Father's path of obedient suffering rather than a crown gained by compromise.
options.note: Each temptation offered glory without the cross, which Jesus rejected.

options.text: He did not want the kingdoms of the world at all.
options.note: He is destined to receive the kingdoms, but from the Father by the way of the cross, not from the devil.

options.text: He was not yet powerful enough to accept them.
options.note: The issue was faithfulness, not a lack of power.

options.text: He doubted that the devil could deliver on the offer.
options.note: He refused on the ground of worshiping God alone, not because he questioned the offer.

questions: Jesus met every temptation with the word of God. How well do you know the Scriptures you would need in your own moments of testing, and how might you hide more of God's word in your heart?
The tempter's refrain was if You are the Son of God, an attack on Jesus' trust in the Father's love just declared at His baptism. Where does the enemy tempt you to doubt what God has said about you?
Each temptation offered a shortcut to something good by a wrong path. Where are you tempted to reach a good goal by a way that dishonors God?
Jesus insisted that worship and service belong to God alone. What rivals for that exclusive worship are competing for your heart right now?
After the trial, angels came and ministered to Jesus. How does it encourage you that God sustains His people in and after their testing?

translationNotes.note: Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 8:3, the lesson Israel was meant to learn in the wilderness, that they live by every word from God's mouth.
The devil quotes Psalm 91:11-12, but misapplies it; Scripture can be twisted, and Jesus answers with Deuteronomy 6:16 against putting God to the test.
Away from Me, Satan and the citation of Deuteronomy 6:13 name the devil plainly and settle that worship belongs to God alone.
